dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for an unconscious patient at Westover Dialysis, in Richmond.
Hello, I'm Ben, a paramedic with the rescue squad. We were called about an emergency here involving an unconscious patient. Can you confirm that?
Yes. She had a seizure, and after that she just didn't wake up. She's completely unresponsive now.
Let me check her responsiveness. Is she opening her eyes or responding to loud noises or touch?
She won't open her eyes, and she's not responding at all-no movement, no signs of awareness.
She's not giving any verbal responses, but we can still keep assessing her for motor reactions. Let me start by testing her pupils with the penlight if you don't mind, Partner.
Her pupils are reactive and following the light. That's still a sign of partial responsiveness.
But overall, I'd rate her as altered mental status. She's not interactive at all aside from those eye movements.
Airway is clear, she appears in normal posture and has no signs of trauma or airway compromise.
She's been placed on a nasal cannula at 2 LPM and is maintaining that oxygen.
Her breathing is consistent with no signs of labored breathing or irregularity. She's taking normal breaths according to what the staff reported.
I'll check and confirm by taking her blood pressure and pulse first.
Blood pressure is 166 over 78. That's within acceptable limits for now.
Pulse is 87, regular for now.
Respiratory rate is at 12 and normal in depth.
Her oxygen saturation is at 99% on the nasal cannula. That seems stable.
I'll take a quick blood sugar now. Let's see if hypoglycemia might be a factor.
Her blood sugar is at 111. Still in the normal range, so hypoglycemia can be ruled out.
Now that her vitals are stable and within acceptable limits, we can proceed to do a 12-lead EKG.
Interpretation of the 12-lead EKG shows sinus rhythm with first-degree AV block. Also showing nonspecific T-wave abnormalities, but no signs of acute myocardial infarction.
Would you be okay with the facility staff providing some medical background? We'll need it for proper documentation.
Yes. She used to have a lot of health issues, including chronic seizures, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a history of stroke due to an infarction.
She also has schizoaffective disorder and is on dialysis three times a week for her end-stage renal failure.
Got it. So, she has a medical background we need to respect-especially her CV conditions like primary hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a history of stroke due to infarction.
When was her last meal or intake before this incident?
She had breakfast a couple of hours ago. It was a small amount, but nothing out of the ordinary.
Are there any medications listed for her, or any known allergics we need to be careful about during this transport?
She's on her dialysis meds, but nothing else as far as current medications. No listed drug allergies either.
Has there been a change in her condition after the dialysis treatment? Any signs that she would not get better this time, like near-fainting or not coming out of the seizure?
No. Her seizures usually pass and she wakes up, but this time she hasn't. They started a nasal cannula, and she remains postictal and unresponsive.
How did this episode specifically present itself at dialysis?
She started having a seizure during the session. That's when they placed her on O2 and called for help.
I'm noting now that the patient has no objective pain rating. There's no movement or signs of discomfort during our check.
We'll skip the full head and neck exam since she's unresponsive and we are under time pressure for transport.
Let me briefly check her skin for injuries or dialysis site signs.
No notable abrasions, rashes, or bite marks. She shows no signs of trauma. Dialysis site seems intact and not inflamed.
Her pulse shows a drop to 70 per minute. Still regular, but notable.
Respiration rate remains at 12 per minute, shallow, but normal.
Blood pressure decreased slightly to 164/77. Still trending stable.
SpO2 is at 100%, still fully saturated.
Her blood sugar still shows no reading. We should continue monitoring.
Let me repeat the EKG to confirm. We saw a first-degree AV block and nonspecific T-wave changes the last time.
No new changes on the EKG. Still shows first-degree block. Same as previous interpretation.
Based on seizure, altered mental status, and medical history, we're initiating the medical-seizure protocol, per Protocol 3-12.
Our strategy is clear: Protocol 3-12. We're stabilizing for transport and monitoring closely.
I'm double-checking. Her pulse now is 78, still regular and steady.
Respiration is 11 per minute. Slightly lower, but still shallow.
Her blood pressure is now at 162/73. Mild fluctuation, but nothing unstable.
SpO2 remains at 100%. She's doing well on oxygen.
Blood sugar still reads 0. We need to check again shortly.
Let me do a second 12-lead EKG as a precaution before we move.
Second EKG is consistent with previous findings. Still normal sinus rhythm and first-degree AV block. No blockages or infarctions.
She's ready for transport. Let's get her secured onto the stretcher and into a semi-Fowler's in the ambulance.
Short transport is ideal. I'll confirm St. Mary's ED is available.
Stretcher is ready. She's now moved and secured in semi-Fowler's position with all seat belts in place.
She's in the ambulance. Let me track her condition during transport.
Let's do another check to confirm she's doing well en route.
Her pulse is at 78 per minute, steady and regulated.
Respiration is 11, shallow but stable.
BP is at 162/73. It's still consistent with previous readings.
SpO2 remains at 100%. Perfect oxygen saturation.
Blood sugar still reads 0, no signs of hypoglycemia.
She's stable and ready for hospital care. Transportation can proceed safely.
All signs point to a safe transport to St. Mary's. Just keep monitoring en route.
We are en route to St. Mary's hospital's ED. Patient remains unresponsive, GCS 4, with stable vitals and ready for hospital assessment.
We have her-vitals are maintained, and the situation is stable. We'll assist as needed upon arrival.
She has a documented history of stroke or CVA due to an infarction which might have contributed or influenced her prolonged postictal state.
Let me check if there's any increase in respiratory workload. She's shallow, unresponsive; so close monitoring is needed during transport.
Can't do a full breath or heart sound check, but I'll do a quick inspection of her chest and make sure it's moving evenly.
No visible deformities, asymmetry, or signs of chest trauma. Her breathing appears consistent.
Let me briefly assess her arm and back for strength and sensation. Dialysis access in the other arm, so it's not possible to feel for pulses on this side.
Her non-dialysis arm has strong peripheral pulses. No obvious deformities, wounds, or signs of trauma.
Quick check on abdomen: soft and non-tender, with normal bowel sounds. No signs of distention or guarding.
No issues reported related to the genitourinary system.
Double-check-respiration 11 per minute, shallow, and regular. Continue monitoring for any increase in work of breathing.
Reaching out to dispatch for confirmation of hospital admission and ED bed availability for St. Mary's.
Hospital confirmed. Patient will be taken to ED Bed 21. No delays expected upon arrival.
All systems go. Prepare for loading into the ambulance now.
Patient is secured and ready for transport. Semi-Fowler's position is stable with oxygen and EKG running.
There are no plans to initiate new interventions. Maintain current stabilization and observation protocols.
Rechecking the caval pulses and skin temperature, seems normal. No signs of shock or circulatory compromise visible.
Her skin is warm, dry, and not cyanotic. Peripheral pulses are strong and regular bilaterally.
Let me watch her chest a bit more. No signs of flaring, use of accessory muscles, or increased effort.
Keep watching EKG closely. First-degree AV block is normal for her but no other changes. Confirmed stable response to initial transport.
Transporting during EKG link to monitor in real-time at the hospital if possible. Let the hospital know we are sending a preliminary interpretation.
She's secured with blue straps. Retractable seat belts in place. All gear is stowed and ready for departure.
Let's keep temperature on file. She's not showing signs of fever and her skin color and warmth is normal.
No underlying rashes, hives, burns, or other skin concerns observed. Dialysis site clean and dry still.
Quick visual check of the head and neck, no signs of deformity or swelling. Scalp intact.
No obvious signs of nuchal rigidity or trauma, but unchanged from previous assessment.
Double-check stretcher and patient location before pulling out. All right, we're clear.
Patient profile just updated in the system. All protocols in place for expedited ED care.
Things are looking stable-no significant issues so far. Let's do another full vitals scan in 5 minutes.
Route to St. Mary's confirmed-traffic seems light, estimated time of arrival is 8 minutes.
She's showing same vitals now-pulse 78, respiratory rate 11, blood pressure 162/73. Oxygen at 100%.
No further interventions planned. She is stable enough for the short transport, with close patient observation.
O2 mask secured with Velcro. EKG lead wires taped to prevent tangling. Redundant connections on the monitor.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: No drainage or hemorrhage observed via the mouth and nasal areas. No signs of obstruction or foreign bodies.
Ambulance is equipped with a stretcher extender for height adjustments, should not be an issue for ED bed transfer.
Hospital is informed of this high-acuity situation and is in the final stages of bed prep for her.
She showed no signs of distress during the last check. Continue high acuity monitoring until arrival.
We confirm location and speed limits. No speed limit overrides, so transporting at normal emergency service speed.
No signs of rectal bleeding or abnormal urinary output. No changes in frequency, urgency, or urine color observed.
She is still completely unresponsive. No signs of pain or restlessness reported from her.
She's en route. ED staff are aware and waiting. We'll communicate in detail upon arrival.
Another round-vitals still trending stably. Check pulse, respiration, and SpO2 one more time before final handover.
Pulses still strong. SpO2 steady at 100%. Blood pressure at 162/73, regular.
We're not adding new meds or procedures. Continuing with basic support and full monitoring.
Quick note-no swelling or dislocation to the neck observed. Chin to chest, soft, no signs of trauma.
We are entering the final block-ambulance camera is on. ED is in view now.
Final check of all monitors and accessories in the ambulance. No systems off or unconnected.
She's still unresponsive upon arrival. All patient conditions stable per our last assessment.
ED arrival confirmed. Let them know she is en route with no major complicating incidents so far.
Bed 21 is her ED destination. We have all records and monitoring data ready for you.
She was unresponsive with GCS of 4 and stable vitals en route. Mild fluctuations in heart rate but not concerning.
Moving her to a flat supine position to facilitate ED transfer and physician team takeover.
She is moved to the hospital ED using the stretcher and EM assist from staff.
She is now in the hospital. We will stay until ED received all documentation and the patient status transition is clear.
All hands clear of the stretcher, and the patient transition is completed. She's in the hands of nursing staff.
We've fulfilled field care duties and completed care upon arrival to St. Mary's ED. We will convey full care plan and EKG details to the staff before leaving.
